questions.stem: A geodesic on a surface is defined as a curve whose:
options: length is minimal among all curves.
questions.explanation: A geodesic is a curve with geodesic curvature $k_g \equiv 0$. Equivalently, its acceleration has no tangential component: the only bending is forced by the surface (the normal part). Shortest paths are geodesics, but the defining property is $k_g = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The geodesic curvature $k_g$ of a curve on a surface measures:
options: the tangential component of the curve's acceleration, that is, how much it turns within the surface.
how fast the surface bends away from its tangent plane.
questions.explanation: Splitting the acceleration of a unit-speed curve into parts normal and tangent to the surface, the tangential part has magnitude $|k_g|$. It records turning measured intrinsically, inside the surface, ignoring the unavoidable bending captured by the normal curvature.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For a unit-speed curve $\gamma$ on a surface, the condition to be a geodesic can be stated as:
options: $\gamma''$ is parallel to the surface normal $N$ at every point (no tangential acceleration).
questions.explanation: A unit-speed curve is a geodesic exactly when its acceleration $\gamma''$ is everywhere normal to the surface, i.e. parallel to $N$. Then the tangential acceleration (the geodesic curvature) vanishes, so the curve goes 'as straight as possible'.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The geodesics of a plane are exactly:
questions.explanation: In the plane the surface normal is constant, so a unit-speed curve has $\gamma'' \parallel N$ only when $\gamma'' = 0$, which means the curve is a straight line. Straight lines are also the shortest paths, as expected.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The geodesics of a sphere are exactly:
questions.explanation: On a sphere the geodesics are the great circles, the intersections with planes through the center. For a great circle the acceleration points toward the center, parallel to the radial normal $N$, so $k_g = 0$. A non-great circle has nonzero geodesic curvature.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On a sphere, which circles of latitude are geodesics?
options: only the polar circles near the top and bottom.
questions.explanation: A circle of latitude is a geodesic only when it is a great circle, which happens solely for the equator. Every other parallel has a center off the sphere's center, so its acceleration is not purely radial and $k_g \ne 0$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The geodesics of a right circular cylinder are:
options: the straight lines, the circles, and the helices.
questions.explanation: Unrolling the cylinder onto a plane (a local isometry) turns geodesics into straight lines. Rolling back, those straight lines become vertical generators, horizontal circles, and helices. All three families are geodesics of the cylinder.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Why are the geodesics of a cylinder helices (in general)?
options: Because a cylinder is not a regular surface.
Because a cylinder is locally isometric to the plane, and straight lines become helices when the plane is rolled up.
Because the Gaussian curvature of a cylinder is negative.
Because the cylinder has constant positive curvature.
questions.explanation: A cylinder has $K = 0$ and unrolls isometrically onto a flat strip. Geodesics are preserved by local isometries, so the plane's straight lines correspond to the cylinder's geodesics. A slanted line wraps into a helix.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Through a given point of a surface, in a given tangent direction, the number of geodesics is:
questions.explanation: The geodesic equations are a second-order ODE system, so by existence and uniqueness there is exactly one geodesic through a point with a prescribed initial velocity direction. This is the geometric content of the geodesic equations.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A defining feature of the geodesic equations is that they depend only on:
options: the coefficients $E, F, G$ of the first fundamental form and their derivatives (intrinsic data).
the embedding of the surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
questions.explanation: The geodesic equations are written using the Christoffel symbols, which are built from $E, F, G$ and their first derivatives. So being a geodesic is an intrinsic property, preserved by isometries and independent of how the surface sits in space.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Geodesics, parametrized by arc length, always have:
questions.explanation: For a geodesic the acceleration is normal to the surface, so $\tfrac{d}{dt}|\gamma'|^2 = 2\langle \gamma'', \gamma'\rangle = 0$ because $\gamma' $ is tangent and $\gamma''$ is normal. Hence the speed is constant; a geodesic is naturally parametrized proportionally to arc length.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The local Gauss-Bonnet theorem, for a simple region $R$ bounded by a positively oriented piecewise-regular curve with exterior angles $\theta_i$, states:
questions.explanation: The local form is $\oint_{\partial R} k_g\, ds + \iint_R K\, dA + \sum_i \theta_i = 2\pi$. It ties the boundary turning ($k_g$ integral plus exterior angles) to the curvature enclosed. The constant $2\pi$ reflects that the region is a topological disk.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The global Gauss-Bonnet theorem for a compact orientable surface $S$ without boundary states:
options: $\displaystyle\iint_S K\, dA = 2\pi\, \chi(S)$, where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic.
questions.explanation: Globally, $\iint_S K\, dA = 2\pi\,\chi(S)$. The total Gaussian curvature is a topological invariant: it does not change when the surface is bent, only when its topology (its Euler characteristic) changes.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The Euler characteristic of the sphere $S^2$ is:
questions.explanation: The sphere has Euler characteristic $\chi(S^2) = 2$ (for a cube, $V - E + F = 8 - 12 + 6 = 2$). Hence $\iint_{S^2} K\, dA = 2\pi \cdot 2 = 4\pi$, independent of how the sphere is deformed.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For any sphere of radius $R$, the total Gaussian curvature $\iint_S K\, dA$ equals:
questions.explanation: On a sphere of radius $R$, $K = 1/R^2$ is constant and the area is $4\pi R^2$, so $\iint_S K\, dA = \tfrac{1}{R^2}\cdot 4\pi R^2 = 4\pi$. This matches $2\pi\chi = 2\pi\cdot 2 = 4\pi$ for every radius.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For a torus $T^2$, the total Gaussian curvature $\iint_T K\, dA$ equals:
questions.explanation: The torus has Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2) = 0$, so $\iint_T K\, dA = 2\pi\cdot 0 = 0$. The positive curvature on the outer part exactly cancels the negative curvature on the inner part.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For a geodesic triangle (all three sides are geodesics) with interior angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ on a surface, Gauss-Bonnet gives:
questions.explanation: Since the sides are geodesics, $\oint k_g\, ds = 0$. Writing exterior angles as $\pi - \alpha$, etc., the local theorem becomes $\iint_R K\, dA + (3\pi - \alpha - \beta - \gamma) = 2\pi$, so $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi + \iint_R K\, dA$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For a geodesic triangle on a flat surface ($K = 0$), the angle sum is:
questions.explanation: With $K = 0$ the term $\iint_R K\, dA$ vanishes, so $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi$. This recovers the familiar Euclidean fact that a triangle's angles sum to $180^\circ$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On a sphere of radius $1$ ($K = 1$), a geodesic triangle with interior angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ has area equal to:
questions.explanation: With $K = 1$, $\iint_R K\, dA = \text{Area}$. From $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi + \text{Area}$ we get $\text{Area} = (\alpha + \beta + \gamma) - \pi$, the spherical excess. The area of a spherical triangle is its angle excess.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A geodesic triangle on the unit sphere has three right angles. Its area is:
questions.explanation: Each angle is $\pi/2$, so the excess is $\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2}+\tfrac{\pi}{2}+\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) - \pi = \tfrac{3\pi}{2} - \pi = \tfrac{\pi}{2}$. With $K = 1$ this excess is the area, and indeed such a triangle is one eighth of the sphere: $\tfrac{1}{8}(4\pi) = \tfrac{\pi}{2}$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On the unit sphere, a geodesic triangle with all three angles equal to $\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ has area:
questions.explanation: The angle sum is $3\cdot\tfrac{2\pi}{3} = 2\pi$, so the excess is $2\pi - \pi = \pi$. With $K = 1$ the area equals this excess, namely $\pi$ (a quarter of the sphere's total area $4\pi$).
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On a surface of negative Gaussian curvature ($K < 0$), the angle sum of a geodesic triangle is:
questions.explanation: Since $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi + \iint_R K\, dA$ and $K < 0$ makes the integral negative, the angle sum is less than $\pi$. Triangles on saddle-like (hyperbolic) surfaces are 'thin'.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On a sphere of radius $R$, a geodesic triangle with angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ has area:
questions.explanation: Here $K = 1/R^2$ is constant, so $\iint_R K\, dA = \tfrac{1}{R^2}\,\text{Area}$. Setting this equal to the excess $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma) - \pi$ gives $\text{Area} = R^2\big[(\alpha+\beta+\gamma) - \pi\big]$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Which property of a curve is preserved by a local isometry between surfaces?
options: the second fundamental form along it.
questions.explanation: Geodesic curvature is intrinsic, computable from $E, F, G$ alone, so being a geodesic is preserved by isometries. Normal curvature, the second fundamental form, and the principal curvatures depend on the embedding and are not intrinsic.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A great circle on the unit sphere divides it into two hemispheres. Considering one hemisphere as a region $R$ with the great circle as a geodesic boundary, the local Gauss-Bonnet theorem gives $\iint_R K\, dA = $
questions.explanation: The boundary is a geodesic, so $\oint k_g\, ds = 0$, and a smooth boundary has no corner angles. Thus $\iint_R K\, dA = 2\pi$. Indeed $K = 1$ and a hemisphere has area $2\pi$, so $\iint_R K\, dA = 2\pi$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On the unit sphere ($K = 1$), consider a geodesic triangle with interior angles $\alpha = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, $\beta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, and $\gamma = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
questions.solution: By Gauss-Bonnet the angle sum equals $\pi$ plus the integral of $K$. The angle sum here is $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$, so the spherical excess (the integral of $K$ over the region) is {{1}} times $\pi$. Since $K = 1$, the area of the triangle equals this excess, and the triangle is exactly {{2}} of the whole sphere.
questions.explanation: Excess $= \tfrac{3\pi}{2} - \pi = \tfrac{\pi}{2} = 0.5\,\pi$. With $K = 1$, area $= \tfrac{\pi}{2}$. The sphere has area $4\pi$, and $\tfrac{\pi/2}{4\pi} = \tfrac{1}{8}$, so the triangle is one eighth of the sphere.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Apply the global Gauss-Bonnet theorem $\iint_S K\, dA = 2\pi\,\chi(S)$ to standard closed surfaces.
questions.solution: The sphere has Euler characteristic $\chi = 2$, so its total Gaussian curvature is {{1}} times $\pi$. The torus has Euler characteristic $\chi =$ {{2}}, so its total Gaussian curvature is $0$. The total curvature of a sphere is therefore {{3}} of how large or small the radius is.
questions.explanation: Sphere: $2\pi\cdot 2 = 4\pi$, so the value is $4\pi$. Torus: $\chi = 0$, giving $2\pi\cdot 0 = 0$. The total curvature $4\pi$ is a topological invariant, independent of the radius.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Classify the standard named geodesics by matching each surface to its geodesics.
questions.solution: The geodesics of a plane are exactly the {{1}}. The geodesics of a sphere are exactly the {{2}}. On a cylinder the geodesics are the straight generators, the circular cross sections, and the {{3}}.

options: spirals that spiral into a point

questions.explanation: Plane: straight lines. Sphere: great circles. Cylinder: vertical lines, horizontal circles, and helices, since unrolling the cylinder is an isometry carrying its geodesics to the plane's straight lines.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: State the local Gauss-Bonnet theorem and read off the geodesic-triangle formula.
questions.solution: For a simple region $R$ with piecewise-regular boundary, $\displaystyle\oint_{\partial R} k_g\, ds + \iint_R K\, dA + \sum_i \theta_i = 2\pi$, where the $\theta_i$ are the {{1}} angles. When all boundary arcs are geodesics, the line integral of $k_g$ is {{2}}. Writing each exterior angle as $\pi$ minus an interior angle then yields, for a geodesic triangle, the angle sum {{3}} plus the integral of $K$.
questions.explanation: The local theorem uses exterior angles $\theta_i$. Geodesic sides give $k_g = 0$, so the line integral is $0$. With exterior angles $\pi - \alpha_i$, the constant $2\pi$ rearranges to $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi + \iint_R K\, dA$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On a sphere of radius $R = 2$, a navigator walks a geodesic triangle whose interior angles sum to $\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$. Find the area enclosed.
questions.solution: The spherical excess is the angle sum minus $\pi$, which equals {{1}} times $\pi$ (give the decimal coefficient). Since $K = 1/R^2$ is constant, the area is $R^2$ times the excess. With $R = 2$ the area equals {{2}} times $\pi$ (decimal). The angle sum exceeds $\pi$, so the triangle sits on a surface of {{3}} curvature.
questions.explanation: Excess $= \tfrac{5\pi}{4} - \pi = \tfrac{\pi}{4} = 0.25\,\pi$. Area $= R^2\cdot\text{excess} = 4\cdot\tfrac{\pi}{4} = \pi = 1\,\pi$. An angle sum above $\pi$ signals positive Gaussian curvature.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Recall what makes geodesics intrinsic and how they behave under arc-length parametrization.
questions.solution: A geodesic is a curve whose geodesic curvature is {{1}} everywhere, equivalently its acceleration is parallel to the surface {{2}}. Because the acceleration is then perpendicular to the velocity, a geodesic has {{3}} speed when traced out.
questions.explanation: Geodesic curvature $k_g = 0$ defines a geodesic, which means $\gamma''$ is parallel to $N$. Then $\tfrac{d}{dt}|\gamma'|^2 = 2\langle\gamma'',\gamma'\rangle = 0$, so the speed is constant.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces
